query: If I had a wind asset in ERCOT West and a solar asset with the same capacity in CAISO SP15 zone in 2024, which one would make me the most merchant revenue assuming real time market prices only and no curtailment in both regions. Use revenue numbers in $/MWh for the comparison
final_answer:
Using 2024 **real-time market prices only** and **no curtailment**, the **ERCOT West wind asset wins on merchant revenue per MWh**.

| Asset | 2024 real-time capture revenue | Source |
|---|---:|---|
| ERCOT West wind | **$18.96/MWh** | Computed from ERCOT real-time LMPs in `ercot_db.ercot_lmp` (`zone_name='west'`, `zone_type='hub'`, `is_day_ahead=false`) and ERCOT West wind actual generation in GridStatus dataset `ercot_wind_actual_and_forecast_by_geo_region_hourly`. |
| CAISO SP15 solar | **$9.47/MWh** | Computed from CAISO real-time 5-minute LMPs at `TH_SP15_GEN-APND` in GridStatus dataset `caiso_lmp_real_time_5_min` and SP15 solar actual generation in GridStatus dataset `caiso_renewables_hourly`; CAISO/ GridStatus identify `TH_SP15_GEN-APND` as the SP15 trading hub. |

**Result:** ERCOT West wind earns about **$9.48/MWh more** than CAISO SP15 solar on this capture-price basis.  
So, **wind would make the higher merchant revenue per MWh** in 2024.  

**Method note:** Because you asked for comparison in **$/MWh**, I used a **capture price** approach:
\[
\text{capture revenue} = \frac{\sum (\text{generation MWh} \times \text{real-time price})}{\sum \text{generation MWh}}
\]
This compares the realized merchant value of the output profile under real-time pricing. Sources: ERCOT table description in `ercot_db.ercot_lmp`, GridStatus dataset metadata for `ercot_wind_actual_and_forecast_by_geo_region_hourly`, `caiso_lmp_real_time_5_min`, and `caiso_renewables_hourly`.

import pandas as pd, numpy as np
# Load files
ercot_wind = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_wind_actual_and_forecast_by_geo_region_hourly_20260619_062334.csv')
ercot_price = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260619_062726.csv')
caiso_solar = pd.read_csv('/work/caiso_renewables_hourly_20260619_062335.csv')
caiso_price = pd.read_csv('/work/caiso_lmp_real_time_5_min_20260619_062634.csv')

# Parse times
for df in [ercot_wind, ercot_price, caiso_solar, caiso_price]:
    for col in df.columns:
        if 'utc' in col or 'time' in col.lower() or 'hour' in col.lower() or 'timestamp' in col.lower():
            try:
                df[col] = pd.to_datetime(df[col], utc=True)
            except Exception:
                pass

# Clean generation
ercot_wind['gen_mw'] = ercot_wind['gen_west'].clip(lower=0)
caiso_solar['solar_mw'] = caiso_solar['solar'].clip(lower=0)

# Determine price column names
print('ercot price columns', ercot_price.columns)
print('caiso price columns', caiso_price.columns)

# Merge inner on UTC hour start
# ERCOT price file has hourly rows maybe already at hour granularity
ercot = ercot_wind.merge(ercot_price[['hour_utc','avg_lmp']], left_on='interval_start_utc', right_on='hour_utc', how='inner')
# CAISO price from 5-min resampled hourly; column interval_start_utc, lmp
caiso = caiso_solar.merge(caiso_price[['interval_start_utc','lmp']], on='interval_start_utc', how='inner')

# Compute weighted average capture price and annual revenue/MW
for name, df, gen_col, price_col in [('ERCOT West wind', ercot, 'gen_mw', 'avg_lmp'), ('CAISO SP15 solar', caiso, 'solar_mw', 'lmp')]:
    df['revenue_$'] = df[gen_col] * df[price_col]
    total_mwh = df[gen_col].sum()  # 1 hour intervals => MW == MWh per hour
    total_rev = df['revenue_$'].sum()
    capture = total_rev / total_mwh if total_mwh else np.nan
    avg_gen = df[gen_col].mean()
    annual_rev_per_mwcap = total_rev / 1.0  # because actual gen series is region MW, not per MW asset
    print('\n', name)
    print('rows', len(df), 'total_mwh', total_mwh, 'total_rev', total_rev, 'capture', capture, 'avg_gen', avg_gen)
    print('first rows', df[['interval_start_utc', gen_col, price_col]].head(3).to_dict('records'))
    print('last rows', df[['interval_start_utc', gen_col, price_col]].tail(3).to_dict('records'))

# Also simple average price for context
print('\nSimple avg ERCOT price', ercot['avg_lmp'].mean())
print('Simple avg CAISO price', caiso['lmp'].mean())
print('Weighted capture ERCOT', (ercot['gen_mw']*ercot['avg_lmp']).sum()/ercot['gen_mw'].sum())
print('Weighted capture CAISO', (caiso['solar_mw']*caiso['lmp']).sum()/caiso['solar_mw'].sum())

steps.content:
Using 2024 **real-time market prices only** and **no curtailment**, the **ERCOT West wind asset wins on merchant revenue per MWh**.

| Asset | 2024 real-time capture revenue | Source |
|---|---:|---|
| ERCOT West wind | **$18.96/MWh** | Computed from ERCOT real-time LMPs in `ercot_db.ercot_lmp` (`zone_name='west'`, `zone_type='hub'`, `is_day_ahead=false`) and ERCOT West wind actual generation in GridStatus dataset `ercot_wind_actual_and_forecast_by_geo_region_hourly`. |
| CAISO SP15 solar | **$9.47/MWh** | Computed from CAISO real-time 5-minute LMPs at `TH_SP15_GEN-APND` in GridStatus dataset `caiso_lmp_real_time_5_min` and SP15 solar actual generation in GridStatus dataset `caiso_renewables_hourly`; CAISO/ GridStatus identify `TH_SP15_GEN-APND` as the SP15 trading hub. |

**Result:** ERCOT West wind earns about **$9.48/MWh more** than CAISO SP15 solar on this capture-price basis.  
So, **wind would make the higher merchant revenue per MWh** in 2024.  

**Method note:** Because you asked for comparison in **$/MWh**, I used a **capture price** approach:
\[
\text{capture revenue} = \frac{\sum (\text{generation MWh} \times \text{real-time price})}{\sum \text{generation MWh}}
\]
This compares the realized merchant value of the output profile under real-time pricing. Sources: ERCOT table description in `ercot_db.ercot_lmp`, GridStatus dataset metadata for `ercot_wind_actual_and_forecast_by_geo_region_hourly`, `caiso_lmp_real_time_5_min`, and `caiso_renewables_hourly`.
